seo.title: Prachuap Khiri Khan Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Complete guide to Prachuap Khiri Khan Province: Hua Hin, Phraya Nakhon Cave, Sam Roi Yot, wild elephants at Kui Buri, and Thailand's narrowest point. Area: 6,368 km², 8 districts. Plan your trip.

provinceNameThai: ประจวบคีรีขันธ์
overview: Prachuap Khiri Khan is a long, narrow coastal province on the Gulf of Thailand in western Thailand, stretching approximately 212 km along the coast. It contains the narrowest point of Thailand, where just 12.38 km separates the Gulf of Thailand from the Myanmar border in the Tenasserim Hills. The province is home to Hua Hin — Thailand's oldest seaside resort town and a royal retreat since the 1920s — as well as pristine national parks including Sam Roi Yot and Kui Buri, where wild elephants roam freely. Despite its proximity to Bangkok (240 km), much of the province retains an authentic Thai coastal character with fishing villages, fresh seafood markets, and uncrowded beaches.
geography: Prachuap Khiri Khan covers 6,368 km² along the Gulf of Thailand coast, making it one of the longest and narrowest provinces in the country. The Tenasserim Hills form the western border with Myanmar's Tanintharyi Region, while the eastern coast faces the Gulf of Thailand. The province occupies the narrowest section of the Isthmus of Kra, where at one point just 12.38 km separates sea from border. The landscape alternates between limestone mountains, mangrove forests, coconut plantations, and pineapple fields (Prachuap is Thailand's largest pineapple producer). Sam Roi Yot ('Three Hundred Peaks') National Park features dramatic limestone karst formations, freshwater marshes, and the famous Phraya Nakhon Cave. The coastline includes long sandy beaches, rocky headlands, and small bays sheltered by forested hills.
highlights: Phraya Nakhon Cave — iconic cave with a royal pavilion illuminated by sunlight streaming through the collapsed ceiling, inside Sam Roi Yot National Park
Hua Hin — Thailand's oldest beach resort town, a royal retreat since the 1920s with night markets, golf courses, and family-friendly beaches
Kui Buri National Park — Thailand's best destination for wild elephant watching, with a 95% sighting success rate on afternoon safaris
Ao Manao — a scenic bay inside a Thai Air Force base, flanked by limestone hills and offering calm turquoise waters
Khao Takiab (Chopsticks Hill) — a hilltop temple at the southern end of Hua Hin beach with panoramic views and resident macaque monkeys
Thailand's narrowest point — only 12.38 km from the Gulf of Thailand to the Myanmar border, a unique geographic landmark
Pranburi mangrove forest — a coastal boardwalk through preserved mangrove ecosystems between Hua Hin and Sam Roi Yot
Rajabhakti Park — monumental bronze statues of seven Thai kings, set against the backdrop of the Tenasserim Hills south of Hua Hin

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Feb: Cool, dry season with temperatures of 24–31°C and minimal rainfall. Ideal for beach visits, national park hikes, and elephant watching at Kui Buri. This is the busiest period for Hua Hin, especially during Thai school holidays and the Christmas/New Year period.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Mar–May: Hot season with temperatures reaching 33–36°C. Seas remain calm, and beaches are less crowded than peak season. Songkran celebrations in Hua Hin (mid-April) are popular. Good value on hotels outside of Thai holiday weekends.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun–Oct: Monsoon season brings afternoon showers and occasional heavy rain, but mornings are often sunny. Sam Roi Yot and Kui Buri are lush and green. Hotel prices drop 20–40% in Hua Hin. September–October is the wettest period.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to February for the best weather and comfortable hiking conditions at Sam Roi Yot. For Phraya Nakhon Cave, visit mid-morning (10–11 AM) when sunlight illuminates the pavilion. Budget travelers should consider June–July when Hua Hin hotel prices drop significantly but weather remains manageable.

topAttractions.name: Phraya Nakhon Cave (Sam Roi Yot National Park)
topAttractions.description: Thailand's most photographed cave, hidden within Sam Roi Yot National Park. Two massive sinkholes in the cave ceiling allow sunlight to illuminate the Kuha Karuhas Pavilion, built in 1890 for King Chulalongkorn (Rama V). The hike involves a steep 430-meter trail from Laem Sala Beach, followed by a challenging climb up to the cave. The best time to visit is 10–11 AM when the sun illuminates the pavilion directly.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB (foreigners), 40 THB (Thais); boat from Bang Pu to Laem Sala: 400 THB round trip

topAttractions.name: Kui Buri National Park
topAttractions.description: Thailand's premier wild elephant watching destination, located about 85 km southwest of Hua Hin. The dedicated wildlife viewing area offers a 95% chance of spotting wild Asian elephants, along with gaur (wild cattle), langurs, and hornbills. Safari pickup trucks with guides depart daily from the park entrance for afternoon game drives between 2 PM and 6 PM.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB (foreigners), 100 THB (children 3–14); safari pickup: 1,000 THB per group (max 6 people)

topAttractions.name: Khao Takiab (Chopsticks Hill)
topAttractions.description: A rocky headland at the southern end of Hua Hin beach, topped by the Buddhist temple Wat Khao Lad and a large standing Buddha statue. The hilltop offers panoramic views over Hua Hin Bay and the coastline. The area is known for its colony of cheeky macaque monkeys — keep belongings secure and avoid feeding them.

topAttractions.name: Ao Manao
topAttractions.description: A crescent-shaped bay with calm turquoise waters, set within the Wing 5 Royal Thai Air Force base south of Prachuap Khiri Khan town. Flanked by limestone hills (Khao Lom Muak and Khao Tat Mook), the beach is popular with Thai families on weekends but quiet during the week. Food stalls serve fresh seafood under covered pavilions across from the beach.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Free (valid ID required for base entry)

topAttractions.name: Hua Hin Night Market
topAttractions.description: A bustling night market along Dechanuchit Road in the heart of Hua Hin, running every evening from around 5 PM to 11 PM. Stalls offer fresh seafood grilled to order (from 40 THB), Thai street food favorites, tropical fruit smoothies, clothing, souvenirs, and handmade crafts. The adjacent Chatchai Market specializes in fresh seafood for cooking at home.

topAttractions.name: Rajabhakti Park
topAttractions.description: A monumental park featuring seven enormous bronze statues (up to 13.9 meters tall) of Thai kings who shaped the nation's history, from King Ramkhamhaeng to King Rama IX. Set against the scenic backdrop of the Tenasserim Hills, the park includes a museum, landscaped gardens, and an evening light show. Located 20 km south of Hua Hin center.

topAttractions.name: Pranburi Forest Park (Mangrove Walk)
topAttractions.description: A coastal mangrove conservation area between Hua Hin and Sam Roi Yot, featuring an elevated boardwalk winding through dense mangrove forest. The park was established in 1982 to protect the ecosystem and offers birdwatching, kayaking through mangrove channels, and views of the Gulf of Thailand. A peaceful half-day trip from Hua Hin.

localFood: Pla Kapong Tod Nam Pla — fried sea bass with fish sauce, a signature dish at seafood restaurants along Prachuap Khiri Khan's waterfront promenade
Pad Thai Goong Sod — Prachuap-style pad thai with fresh Gulf shrimp, cooked on massive flat-top grills at local market stalls
Hoy Kraeng Luak (blanched cockles) — freshly harvested cockles served with spicy seafood dipping sauce, a Gulf coast specialty
Poo Nim Tod Krob — deep-fried soft-shell crab, crispy and served whole with sweet chili sauce, popular at Hua Hin's night market
Kaeng Som Pla — sour curry with fresh fish, tamarind, and local vegetables; a staple of the province's coastal cooking tradition
Sap-pa-rot Prachuap (Prachuap pineapple) — the province is Thailand's largest pineapple producer, with roadside stalls selling fresh-cut fruit for 20–30 THB
Kuai Tiao Kung Mangkon (lobster noodles) — rice noodle soup with local lobster, available at seafood restaurants in Bang Saphan and Prachuap town
culture: Prachuap Khiri Khan's culture is deeply tied to the sea, with fishing communities defining the character of towns like Prachuap, Pranburi, and Bang Saphan. The province holds special royal significance — King Rama VII built Klai Kangwon Palace ('Far from Worries') in Hua Hin in 1929, and the Thai royal family continues to use it as a retreat. This royal connection has influenced the town's development as a refined, family-oriented resort destination rather than a party destination. The province also played a significant historical role during World War II, as Japanese forces landed at several beaches along the coast on December 8, 1941, meeting fierce resistance from Thai forces at Ao Manao — commemorated annually at the Wing 5 Air Force base. Local festivals include the Prachuap Khiri Khan City Pillar Shrine Festival and the Hua Hin Jazz Festival.
gettingThere: By Train: Southern Line trains from Bangkok's Hua Lamphong station run approximately 10 times daily. Express trains reach Hua Hin in 3.5–4 hours (from 252 THB) and Prachuap Khiri Khan town in 5 hours (from 350 THB). By Bus: Buses depart hourly from Bangkok's Sai Tai Mai (Southern) Bus Terminal, taking 3.5–4 hours to Hua Hin (200–320 THB) and 5 hours to Prachuap town. By Car: Drive south on Highway 4 (Phetkasem Road) from Bangkok — approximately 200 km to Hua Hin, 280 km to Prachuap town. By Minivan: Minivans from Victory Monument in Bangkok to Hua Hin take 3 hours (180–250 THB). Note: There is no commercial airport in the province; the nearest airports are Hua Hin Airport (limited domestic flights) and U-Tapao (Pattaya) or Suvarnabhumi (Bangkok).
gettingAround: Songthaew (shared pickup trucks) run fixed routes within Hua Hin for 10–20 THB. Motorbike taxis are common for short trips (20–60 THB). In Prachuap town, tuk-tuks charge 40–60 THB to Ao Manao. Motorbike rental is the most flexible option for exploring the province (200–300 THB/day, international license required). Bicycle rental is available in Prachuap town for 50 THB/day. Grab ride-hailing is available in Hua Hin. For reaching Sam Roi Yot and Kui Buri national parks, a private car or motorbike is recommended as public transport options are limited. Local songthaews connect Hua Hin to Pranburi (40 THB, 30 min).
travelTips: Visit Phraya Nakhon Cave between 10–11 AM for the iconic sunbeam illuminating the royal pavilion — arrive at Bang Pu pier by 8:30 AM to allow time for the boat and hike
Kui Buri elephant safaris run from 2–6 PM only — book a safari pickup at the park entrance by 2 PM for the best wildlife sighting chances
Ao Manao beach is inside a military base — bring valid ID (passport for foreigners) as it is checked at the gate
Hua Hin is a popular Thai weekend destination — visit Monday to Thursday for lower hotel prices and less crowded beaches
The train from Bangkok to Hua Hin is scenic and affordable but the air-conditioned sprinter service is fastest (3.5 hours vs. 5+ for ordinary trains)
Prachuap Khiri Khan town is far more authentic and less touristy than Hua Hin — worth an overnight stay for fresh seafood and waterfront dining
Bring reef-safe sunscreen and sturdy shoes for Sam Roi Yot — the hike to Phraya Nakhon Cave involves steep, rocky terrain
The province stretches over 200 km north-south — allow multiple days if planning to visit both Hua Hin (north) and Prachuap/Kui Buri (south)

sources.sourceName: Thai National Parks — Sam Roi Yot
